coredata.dc:title: Boosting visible-light driven solar-fuel production over g-C3N4/tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride hybrid photocatalyst via incorporation with carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xuehua
Lin, Lin
Qu, Dan
Yang, Jiangong
Weng, Yuxiang
Wang, Zhuan
Sun, Zaicheng
Chen, Yong
He, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Developing highly efficient photocatalysts for solar-fuels production under visible-light irradiation is of great promise, but still remains a big challenge. In this work, we use g-C3N4 incorporated with carbon dots as the photosensitizer to couple with tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride (FeTCPP) molecular catalyst. The obtained g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalyst exhibits high activity for the solar-fuels production of CO and H2 under visible-light irradiation, with CO yield of 23.1 mmol/g and H2 yield of 71.1 mmol/g in 6 h. The enhanced mechanism has been studied by various techniques like photocurrent response, photoluminescence and mid-infrared femtosecond transient absorption. The presence of trace amount of carbon dots in the system can provide an alternative channel for the electron transfer, i.e., from g-C3N4 to FeTCPP via the CDs. Accordingly, the interfacial charge separation and electron transfer can be promoted, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
g-C3N4
Tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride
Charge separation
Interfacial electron transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure, morphology and absorption characteristics of g-C3N4-Cx
Solar-fuels production over g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts
The function of CDs in charge separation and transfer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.
(a–f) SEM images of as-synthesized g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples, representative image of (g) TEM and (h) HR-TEM for g-C3N4-C0.09, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectra and (b) Tauc plots of the as-prepared g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.

(a) Gas evolution yield and (b) TON value over g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP photocatalysts (50 mg g-C3N4-Cx +0.25 mg of FeTCPP, x = 0∼0.4) within 6 h in CH3CN:H2O:TEOA (3:1:1, 100 mL) mixed solution under visible-light illumination (420 nm < λ < 780 nm), light intensity =220 mW cm−2, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.
Transient photocurrent response of the as-prepared g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) films, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.
PL spectra of (a) g-C3N4-Cx and (b) g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP (x = 0∼0.07) samples in DMF solution excited at 370 nm, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea.
MIR fs-TAS for (a) g-C3N4 and (b) g-C3N4-C0.05 irradiated with pulses at 400 nm, and (c) MIR fs-TAS decay kinetics at 5000 nm of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 powder samples.
Fitted time constant from the MIR fs-TAS decay at 5000 nm for g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 samples.

head.ce:title: Boosting visible-light driven solar-fuel production over g-C3N4/tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride hybrid photocatalyst via incorporation with carbon dots

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: X.H. Zhang and L. Lin contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts exhibit high activity for the solar-fuels production under visible-light irradiation by virtue of the enhanced charge separation and electron transfer due to the incorporation of carbon dots into g-C3N4.

Developing highly efficient photocatalysts for solar-fuels production under visible-light irradiation is of great promise, but still remains a big challenge. In this work, we use g-C3N4 incorporated with carbon dots as the photosensitizer to couple with tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride (FeTCPP) molecular catalyst. The obtained g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalyst exhibits high activity for the solar-fuels production of CO and H2 under visible-light irradiation, with CO yield of 23.1 mmol/g and H2 yield of 71.1 mmol/g in 6 h. The enhanced mechanism has been studied by various techniques like photocurrent response, photoluminescence and mid-infrared femtosecond transient absorption. The presence of trace amount of carbon dots in the system can provide an alternative channel for the electron transfer, i.e., from g-C3N4 to FeTCPP via the CDs. Accordingly, the interfacial charge separation and electron transfer can be promoted, leading to enhanced photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic conversion of carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) into solar fuels and other value-added chemicals is a desirable way to mitigate carbon emission, which can realize the recycling of carbon resources in theory [1–5]. Considerable efforts have been made to develop various catalysts for CO2 photoreduction in the past decades, mainly based on the semiconductors, metal organic frameworks and metal molecular complexes [6–13]. However, CO2 photoreduction is a multi-electron transfer process boosted by the catalysts, which is extremely challenging owing to the intrinsic stability of CO2 and poor product selectivity. Many products (such as CO, HCOOH, HCHO, CH3OH and CH4) can be obtained via the two-, four-, six-, and eight-electron pathway reactions [14,15]. Molecular catalysts based on metal complexes have gained much attention due to their multiple accessible redox states, which are favorable of the multi-electron transfer for CO2 reduction [16–18]. It is noted that the performance for a single component catalyst to photocatalytically reduce CO2 is usually very poor, as it scarcely meets all prerequisites of the complex processes, including broad spectral absorption, effective charge separation, favorable CO2 adsorption and activation, and selective CO2 reduction. Thus, it is urgent and critical to develop highly active and selective hybrid catalytic systems for the CO2 photoreduction.

Given the high catalytic activity of metal molecular complex and high stability and light-harvesting performance of some semiconductors, semiconductor/metal molecular complex hybrid systems can have the strengths of both metal molecular complex and semiconductor [6,19–21]. Various such hybrid photocatalysts have been prepared to improve the CO2 photoreduction performance [22–25]. Iron porphyrins usually exhibit high activity for CO2 photoreduction, but low stability under long-term UV illumination and inactive under visible-light irradiation in case without a photosensitizer [12,26,27]. We have developed some semiconductor/iron porphyrin complex hybrid photocatalysts for CO2 reduction, such as graphitic carbon nitride/tetra(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin iron(III) chloride (g-C3N4/FeTCPP) [28], CdS/FeTCPP [29], ZnxCd1-xS/FeTCPP [30], and CdS/Bi2S3/FeTCPP hybrids [31]. The semiconductors usually act as light absorber in such hybrid systems, while the metal molecular complexes serve as catalytic center for the CO2 reduction reaction and, thus, the light harvesting is perfectly separated from catalytic reaction spatially and temporally. Therefore, semiconductor/metal molecular complex hybrid systems can efficiently improve the CO2 photoreduction performance.

As a typical metal-free semiconductor, g-C3N4 has received much attention in the field of photocatalysis by virtue of its low cost, facile synthesis, suitable band structure and good stability [32–34]. However, photocatalytic reduction of CO2 over pure g-C3N4 is still of great challenge due to the low electronic conductivity and high recombination of charge carriers [35]. Recently transient spectroscopic study has demonstrated that the excessive electron accumulation can induce accelerated recombination loss and, thus, being the photocatalytic efficiency bottleneck of g-C3N4 photocatalysts [36]. Accordingly, many strategies have been employed to improve the conductivity and promote the charge separation of g-C3N4, such as doping elements, depositing quantum dots and metal nanoparticles, and constructing heterostructure [37]. Alternatively, it can be coupled with the metal molecular complexes to fabricate g-C3N4/metal molecular complex hybrid systems. The efficient charge separation and electron transfer from g-C3N4 to the metal molecular complexes in such hybrid systems are extremely crucial to achieve highly efficient CO2 reduction. In addition, it is noted that carbon dots (CDs) with diameter less than 10 nm exhibit good electric conductivity and electron reservoir property [38]. The g-C3N4-CDs (g-C3N4-C) composite has been employed as an efficient photocatalyst for overall water splitting under visible light, which exhibits high charge transfer and separation efficiency [39,40].

Here we used the metal-free composites, g-C3N4 loaded with very small amount of CDs (g-C3N4-Cx), as the photosensitizer to integrate with FeTCPP molecular complex (g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP) for CO2 reduction into solar fuels of CO and H2 under visible-light irradiation. The g-C3N4-Cx were synthesized via one-step homogeneous thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea [40]. The amount of loaded CDs was tuned by changing the quantity of citric acid precursor. The influence of loaded CDs on the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrids for CO2 reduction was systematically studied by using transient photocurrent response, steady state photoluminescence (PL) and mid-infrared femtosecond transient absorption spectroscopy (MIR fs-TAS). Trace amount of CDs in g-C3N4 can greatly promote the charge separation and interfacial electron transfer from g-C3N4 to FeTCPP, resulting in enhanced photoreduction of CO2 over g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts.

All the chemicals and solvents were chromatographic or analytical grade. N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and tetraethylammonium tetrafluoroborate (Et4NBF4) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Triethanolamine (TEOA), acetonitrile, urea and citric acid were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-chem Technology. Carbon dioxide (CO2, super grade purity 99.999 %) was bought from Beijing Beiwen Gases Company. Ultrapure water (Millipore Milli-Q grade, 18.2 MΩ cm) was used in all the experiments.

Typically, the g-C3N4 was synthesized by thermal polymerization of urea, simply heated 10 g of urea to 600 °C in a lidded alumina crucible in muffle furnace with a ramp rate of 0.5 °C min―1, and kept at this temperature for 3 h in ambient atmosphere. The resultant powders were washed with water, HCl, NaOH and once again with water, and finally were dried overnight in a vacuum oven at 65 °C.

The g-C3N4-Cx samples were prepared through a modified one-step homogeneous thermal pyrolysis of citric acid and urea powders together under controlled temperature according to previous method, in which citric acid was the precursor for forming CDs and urea was the precursor for g-C3N4 [40]. The amount of CDs in the final g-C3N4-Cx composites was modulated via changing the quantity of citric acid. For typical synthesis of g-C3N4-C0.05, 10 g of urea and 5 mg of citric acid (weight percentage of citric acid to urea, 0.05 %) were heated using the same protocol as that used for the synthesis of g-C3N4, as mentioned above. The g-C3N4-Cx synthesized with citric acid percentage of 0.03 %, 0.07 %, 0.09 % and 0.4 % was denoted as g-C3N4-C0.03, g-C3N4-C0.07, g-C3N4-C0.09 and g-C3N4-C0.4, respectively.

FeTCPP was synthesized and characterized as previously reported [28,41]. The g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP heterogeneous catalysts were prepared by integrating 0.25 mg of FeTCPP with 50 mg g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) via mechanical mixing under stirring in 100 mL solution of acetonitrile/water/TEOA (3:1:1, v:v:v).

The morphology was observed with Hitachi S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) and FEI Tecnai G2 T20 transmission electron microscope (TEM). Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was measured on Bruker D8 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area was obtained by a surface area and porosity analyzer (Micromeritics, Tristar II 3020). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on Lambda 750 UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA) using BaSO4 as background. PL spectra excited at 370 nm were collected on a NanoLOG-TCSPC spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, USA). Transient photocurrent response curves were measured in a three-electrode cell using CHI 660D potentiostat (Shanghai Chenhua) with a frequency of 1 kHz in DMF solution containing 0.1 M of Et4NBF4 as the electrolyte. The as-prepared films on FTO, Pt plate and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used respectively as the working, counter and reference electrodes.

CO2 photoreduction was carried out in a photoreaction system (Labsolar-IIIAG, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.), using TEOA as sacrificial agent saturated with 28 kPa CO2 at 15 °C as reported previously [28]. The whole system was first vacuumed and purged with Ar for three times to remove the air. Before illumination, the system was purged with high purity CO2 gas for at least 1.5 h. The reactor was illuminated with a 300 W Xe lamp (Microsolar 300, Beijing Perfect Light Technology Co., Ltd.) with a 420 nm cut-off filter and an IR cut-off filter, (420 nm < λ < 780 nm). The light intensity was 220 mW cm―2. The gas products were analyzed by using Agilent 7890A gas chromatography equipped with a hydrogen flame ionized detector (FID) and a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) using He as the carrier gas. A series of control experiments (i.e., purging Ar instead of CO2, under dark without illumination, only solvent without the photocatalyst and only FeTCPP catalyst) were carried out to ensure that the observed products come solely from the CO2 photoreduction (Table S1). The results of control experiments also indicate that the g-C3N4-Cx is the light-harvesting unit and FeTCPP is the catalytic center.

The MIR fs-TAS of g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 powder samples were measured with a home built TAS setup. The light source is a commercial femtosecond amplifier laser system (Spitfire Ace, Spectra Physics, 35 fs, 800 nm, 1 KHz). The laser light was split into two beams, one was used to generate pump pulse (400 nm) with a BBO crystal by second harmonic generation and another was used to generate probe pulse (5000 nm) through four wave mixing process [42,43]. The time delay between the pump and probe pulses was controlled by a motorized translation stage. The transmitted probe light after sample was collected by a laser frequency synchronized a liquid-nitrogen-cooled 64-element mercury-cadmium-telluride array coupled spectrometer (FPAS-0144, Infrared System Development Corporation). Pump intensity was set to be 1.5 mJ cm―2 during the experiments. The detection wavelength range is 4700∼5300 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure, morphology and absorption characteristics of g-C3N4-Cx
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples, where x is the weight percentage of citric acid to urea. All XRD patterns of g-C3N4-Cx samples exhibit the characteristics of g-C3N4 crystal structure with 2θ = 12.8° and 27.8°, corresponding to the respective plane of (100) and (002) crystal planes of g-C3N4. Compared with g-C3N4 prepared form pure urea, the peak at 27.8° originated from the periodic stacking of layers in g-C3N4 nanosheets has no significant shift; while it becomes slight broader from g-C3N4-C0.03 to g-C3N4-C0.4, indicating that more citric acid amount can result in less layers in the (002) direction [44]. However, the peak belonging to CDs (2θ = 26°) is not obvious in the XRD pattern because of the low loading amount of CDs.

The morphology of g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples were investigated by SEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a–f, all the g-C3N4-Cx samples have similar mesoporous structure with very thin nanosheets feature. This indicates that the introduction of citric acid during synthesis cannot destroy the structure of g-C3N4, as supported by the XRD results (Fig. 1). It is known that such thin nanosheet structure is in favor of charge transfer. Fig. 2g shows the representative TEM image of the as-prepared g-C3N4-C0.09, which exhibits flaky and ultrathin nanosheet structure. HR-TEM image reveals that the embedded CDs with an average particle size of 2.3 nm and lattice spacing of 0.24 nm (Fig. 2h), consistent with the (100) crystal plane of the graphite CDs. This is a visual proof that the CDs have been successfully embedded in g-C3N4, indicating the formation of g-C3N4-Cx composites. All the g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples have a relative high BET specific surface area (77.5 ∼ 85.5 m2 g―1, Table S2) due to the ultrathin flaky and porous structure of the g-C3N4 matrix, which slightly changes among the g-C3N4-Cx samples with different amount of CDs.

Fig. 3 a shows the UV–vis DRS spectra of the as-prepared g-C3N4-Cx samples, in which the absorption edge gradually red shifts towards the visible region from g-C3N4 to g-C3N4-C0.4, as the incorporation amount of CDs into g-C3N4 increases. It is noted that two new absorption peaks appear at 475 and 525 nm as the CDs amount increases; meanwhile the baseline increases significantly in the visible region. This phenomenon is due to the CDs prepared from bottom-up approach, giving rise to plenty of defects in g-C3N4. The absorption edge of these g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) samples can be determined to be 3.10∼2.08 eV in terms of the Kubelka-Munk method (Fig. 3b). That is to say, incorporation of CDs into g-C3N4 can lead to gradual red shift in the absorption edge, mainly caused by extended conjugation length because of the introduced π-conjugated sp2 hybrid domain of CDs chemically boned to the melon units of g-C3N4, different from that for the mechanical mixture of g-C3N4 and CDs [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-fuels production over g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the conduction band potential of g-C3N4 nanosheets is negative enough (∼ −1.3 V vs. NHE) [45], it is kinetically feasible for the electrons photogenerated in g-C3N4 to transfer to FeTCPP (∼ −1.02 V at pH 7 vs. NHE) to participate in the reduction reactions. So g-C3N4 acts as the light harvesting unit and FeTCPP works as the catalytic center for the reduction reactions in the hybrid systems. Here turnover number (TON, moleCO/moleFeTCPP and moleH2/moleFeTCPP) is defined as the ratio of the produced amount of CO and H2 to the initially used amount of FeTCPP. The amount of FeTCPP (0.25 mg) is used to calculate the product yield since it acts as the catalytic center. Fig. 4 a shows the evolution yield of CO and H2 within 6 h over the as-prepared g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP (x = 0∼0.4, 50 mg g-C3N4-Cx +0.25 mg of FeTCPP) under visible-light illumination (420∼780 nm), and Fig. 4b shows the corresponding TON values. All the g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts exhibit very high photocatalytic reduction activity for the solar-fuels production of CO and H2, with trace amount of CH4; while no liquid products can be detected. H2 evolution is mainly due to in-situ generation of the protonated TEOA (HOCH2CH2)3NH+, TEOA+), the pKa of which is about 7.8. The acidic TEOA+ is in favor of the formation of porphyrin hydride, leading to the H2 evolution [12].

It is found that the respective CO yield and TONCO value over the g-C3N4/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalyst is 7.6 mmol/g and 6.7, with the H2 yield of 24.4 mmol/g and TONH2 value of 21.9. When the CDs are introduced into the system, the photocatalytic performance first considerately increases for both CO and H2 evolution with increasing amount of CDs. The CO evolution yield reaches the highest value (28.3 mmol/g) in 6 h with TONCO of 24.9 when x = 0.07 (i.e., g-C3N4-C0.07/FeTCPP); while the highest H2 evolution yield is observed (71.1 mmol/g) when x = 0.05 (g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP) in 6 h with TONH2 of 62.6. After the photocatalytic performance reaches the climax, it starts to decline when the loading amount of CDs further increases, and even becomes inferior to the g-C3N4/FeTCPP when x = 0.4 (g-C3N4-C0.4/FeTCPP). The sequenced activity for the CO evolution under visible-light irradiation is g-C3N4-C0.07/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.03/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.09/FeTCPP > g-C3N4/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.4/FeTCPP; while for H2 evolution it is g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.07/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.03/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.09/FeTCPP > g-C3N4/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.4/FeTCPP. It is found that the CO yield over g-C3N4-C0.07/FeTCPP is about 3.7 times of that over g-C3N4/FeTCPP, and the H2 yield over g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP is about 2.9 times of that over g-C3N4/FeTCPP.

Considering both the CO and H2 evolution are two-electron reduction processes, the sum of production yield (i.e., CO + H2) can be used to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of the obtained catalysts, for which the sequenced activity follows g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.07/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.03/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.09/FeTCPP > g-C3N4/FeTCPP > g-C3N4-C0.4/FeTCPP (Fig. 4). In other words, the g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalyst produces the highest total solar fuels yield in 6 h (94.2 mmol/g) with TONCO+H2 value of 82.9, which is about 2.94 times of those for g-C3N4/FeTCPP. In addition, the CO2 photoreduction was also carried out for 12 h using g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid catalyst. The CO and H2 yield increases linearly within 12 h (Fig. S1), indicating the good stability of g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid catalyst under visible-light irradiation.

Such photocatalytic performance of the obtained hybrid catalysts can be indirectly proved by the results of transient photocurrent response (Fig. 5 ), which was carried out in DMF solution with 0.1 M Et4NBF4 using the as-prepared g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.4) films as the electrodes. All of the g-C3N4-Cx films display relatively good stability upon several runs of the measurements. The g-C3N4-C0.05 exhibits the largest photocurrent response under visible-light illumination (420 nm < λ < 780 nm), followed by g-C3N4-C0.07, g-C3N4-C0.03, g-C3N4-C0.09, g-C3N4-C0.4 and g-C3N4. It is noted that g-C3N4-C0.05 and g-C3N4-C0.07 show much larger photocurrent response than the other samples. So loading CDs can greatly enhance the photoelectric performance of g-C3N4. All these agree well with the aforementioned results for solar-fuels production (Fig. 4), except that g-C3N4-C0.4 shows larger photocurrent than g-C3N4 but less solar-fuels production of CO and H2. This may be because other minor reduction products like CH4 can be formed during the solar-fuels production, while they are not counted in the above discussion since their yield is very low. In other words, the g-C3N4-C0.4/FeTCPP may exhibit higher photocatalytic performance than g-C3N4/FeTCPP if the entire reduction products could be detected and considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The function of CDs in charge separation and transfer
ce:section.ce:para: Steady state PL spectra were first collected to study the behavior of photoinduced charge carriers, as the charge separation (recombination) and transfer play a critical role in the photocatalysts. Unfortunately, the samples with a high loading amount of CDs (such as g-C3N4-C0.09 and g-C3N4-C0.4) exhibit poor dispersibility in the solution, which are readily to subside. So here the PL spectra were recorded in DMF solution excited at 370 nm only for the samples of g-C3N4, g-C3N4-C0.03, g-C3N4-C0.05 and g-C3N4-C0.07.

Clearly, all the samples exhibit broad emission at around 450 nm (Fig. 6 ). It is found that the incorporation of CDs into the system can lead to considerable decline in the PL intensity, regardless of the presence of FeTCPP or not. Moreover, the intensity decreases with increased CDs loading amount for the g-C3N4-Cx (x = 0∼0.07) samples (Fig. 6a). Similar to the absorption spectra (Fig. 3a), a red shift also occurs in the emission peak as the CDs loading increases due to the red shift in the absorption edge. Hence, the radiation recombination of photoinduced charge carriers of g-C3N4 can be suppressed greatly by incorporation of CDs. In addition, it is found that the PL intensity of g-C3N4-Cx samples can be further quenched to some degree by introducing FeTCPP molecular catalyst (Fig. 6b). The more FeTCPP in the system, the lower the PL intensity is. All these may imply that the recombination of charge carriers is further suppressed due to the presence of FeTCPP and, especially, the electrons can efficiently transfer from g-C3N4-Cx to FeTCPP.

Specifically, MIR fs-TAS is carried out to further study the function of CDs in the g-C3N4-Cx, as it can be used to evaluate the dynamics of photogenerated charge carriers. Fig. 7 a and b shows the MIR fs-TAS for g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 irradiated with pulse at 400 nm. Obviously, both g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 show relatively strong absorption upon excitation at 400 nm within the mid-IR region of 4700∼5300 nm, which can be ascribed to the trapped electrons [46]. The decay kinetics of photogenerated electrons in g-C3N4 and g-C3N4-C0.05 are tested by recording the changes in transient absorption intensity at 5000 nm after photoexcitation (Fig. 7c). Such decay is fitted by the multi-exponential function, and resultant fitting time constants are summarized in Table 1 .

It is found that the g-C3N4-C0.05 exhibits faster decay than the g-C3N4; while the τ3 of g-C3N4-C0.05 (2224 ps) is almost the same as that of g-C3N4 (2243 ps), possibly corresponding to the long-lived nanosecond scale electrons. Both τ1 (2.9 ps) and τ2 (94 ps) of g-C3N4-C0.05 are much shorter than those of g-C3N4 (5.3 ps for τ1 and 126 ps for τ2), suggesting increased population in both free and surface shallow-trapped electrons in the g-C3N4-C0.05 sample, as these electrons have much shorter lifetime than the deep-trapped electrons. This may imply that the photogenerated electrons can transfer from the g-C3N4 to CDs as the CDs are chemically boned to the melon units of g-C3N4 [40], which is also closely related to the properties of good electric conductivity and electron reservoir of the CDs. Similar phenomena have been observed in BP/g-C3N4 [47] and Pt-TiO2 systems [48] too. Considering the declined PL intensity upon incorporation of CDs and the above results of photoreduction and photocurrent response, it is suggested that an additional channel for electron transfer from g-C3N4 to FeTCPP can thus be feasible to be the one via the CDs, besides the direct pathway.

Therefore, the observed photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts can be explained. Once the CDs are incorporated into the g-C3N4 to form g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP, the photogenerated electrons in g-C3N4 rapidly migrate to the CDs, and then to FeTCPP to take part in the reduction reactions. In other words, the CDs can promote the interfacial separation and transfer of free and surface shallow-trapped electrons from the light-harvesting center of g-C3N4 to the catalytic center of FeTCPP, leading to the enhanced photocatalytic activity. When the loading amount of CDs is low (such as x < 0.07 in g-C3N4-Cx), this positive enhancement effect increases with increasing amount of CDs, i.e., photocatalytic performance increases with increasing amount of CDs. It should be pointed out that here the light absorption of CDs is ignored compared with the g-C3N4 for g-C3N4-Cx (x ≤ 0∼0.09) samples, as its loading amount is very low.

However, further increasing the loading amount of CDs in the g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalysts can lead to substantial decrease in the photocatalytic performance. Similar phenomena have been reported previously [40]. Though the related mechanism is still unclear, we suggest that several factors can have an impact on this. For instance, a large amount of CDs present in the g-C3N4-Cx can hinder the efficient adsorption of FeTCPP, and may also block the light absorption of g-C3N4 to some degree. Moreover, the holes left in g-C3N4 upon photoexcitation will mainly participate in the oxidation half reaction with TEOA if the loading amount of CDs is very low; while plenty of them may also be able to transfer to the CDs due to the good electric conductivity of the CDs if its loading amount is relatively large (such as x > 0.07 in g-C3N4-Cx), resulting in serious recombination of the charge carriers in the CDs and, accordingly, decreased photocatalytic performance of the g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP. As for the light absorption of CDs in case its amount is large, it is not considered here since its photocatalytic activity is low due to its strong photoluminescence performance; while further investigation is expected in future to figure this out.

In summary, we have successfully incorporated trace amount of CDs with g-C3N4 and fabricated g-C3N4-Cx/FeTCPP heterogeneous hybrid photocatalysts for highly efficient production of the solar fuels (CO and H2) under visible-light illumination. The results of photocurrent response, PL spectra and MIR fs-TAS indicate that appropriate amount of CDs incorporated in g-C3N4 can facilitate the interfacial charge separation and electron transfer from light harvesting center g-C3N4 to catalytic center FeTCPP, resulting in improved photocatalytic performance. The g-C3N4-C0.05/FeTCPP hybrid photocatalyst exhibits the highest yield of total CO and H2 (94.2 mmol/g) and TON value (82.9) after 6-h visible-light irradiation, which is nearly 3 times of those for the g-C3N4/FeTCPP.
X.H. Zhang is the major implementer of the research. L. Lin synthesized some catalysts and did the photocatalysis experiments as well as some characterization experiments.
D. Qu combined the g-C3N4 with carbon dots, supervised by Z.C. Sun.
J.G. Yang synthesized the molecular catalyst, supervised by Y. Chen.
Y.X. Weng and Z. Wang was in charge of the transient absorption spectroscopy measurements.
T. He supervised the entire research and wrote the paper together with X.H. Zhang.
All authors contributed to the discussion and provided feedback on the manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21673052, 21972029) and the Belt and Road Initiative by Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2020.118595.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
Stability experiment, control experiments for CO2 photoreduction, and BET specific surface area of the g-C3N4-Cx samples.
